name: logilab-constraint
documentation:
This package implements an extensible constraint satisfaction problem
solver written in pure Python, using constraint propagation algorithms.
The logilab.constraint module provides finite domains with arbitrary
values, finite interval domains, and constraints which can be applied
to variables linked to these domains.

It requires python 2.6 or later to work, and is released under the GNU
Lesser General Public License.

The documentation is in the doc/ directory. Examples are in the
examples/ directory.

Discussion about constraint should take place on the python-projects
mailing list. Information on subscription and mailing list archives can
be accessed at
https://lists.logilab.org/mailman/listinfo/python-projects/

Your feedback is very valuable to us. Please share your experience with
other users of the package on the mailing list.

modules.name: logilab.constraint.fd
documentation:
Tools to work with finite domain variables and constraints

This module provides the following usable classes:
 * FiniteDomain: a class for storing FiniteDomains
 * Expression: a constraint represented as an expression
 * BinaryExpression: a binary constraint represented as an expression
 * various BasicConstraint classes

The Expression and BinaryExpression classes can be constructed using the
make_expression factory function.

method to implement in concrete class

distribute the domains and return each modified domain

Implements DistributorInterface but abstract because
_distribute is left unimplemented.

classes.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.NaiveDistributor

methods.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.NaiveDistributor._distribute
documentation: See AbstractDistributor

distributes domains by splitting the smallest domain in 2 new domains
The first new domain has a size of one,
and the second has all the other values

classes.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.RandomizingDistributor

methods.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.RandomizingDistributor._distribute
documentation: See AbstractDistributor

distributes domains as the NaiveDistrutor, except that the unique
value of the first domain is picked at random.

classes.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.SplitDistributor

methods.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.SplitDistributor.nb_subdomains
documentation: See AbstractDistributor

methods.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.SplitDistributor._distribute
documentation: See AbstractDistributor

distributes domains by splitting the smallest domain in
nb_subspaces equal parts or as equal as possible.
If nb_subspaces is 0, then the smallest domain is split in
domains of size 1

classes.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.DichotomyDistributor
documentation:
distributes domains by splitting the smallest domain in
two equal parts or as equal as possible

classes.name: logilab.constraint.distributors.EnumeratorDistributor
documentation:
distributes domains by splitting the smallest domain
in domains of size 1.

ensures that the domains are consistent with the constraint

methods.name: logilab.constraint.fi.FiniteIntervalDomain.__init__
documentation:
lowestMin is the lowest value of a low boundary for a variable (inclusive).
highestMax is the highest value of a high boundary for a variable (exclusive).
min_length is the minimum width of the interval.
max_length is the maximum width of the interval.
           Use None to have max = min.
resolution is the precision to use for constraint satisfaction. Defaults to 1

methods.name: logilab.constraint.fi.FiniteIntervalDomain.size
documentation: computes the size of a finite interval

methods.name: logilab.constraint.fi.FiniteIntervalDomain.copy
documentation: clone the domain
Domain for a variable with interval values.

classes.name: logilab.constraint.fi.FiniteIntervalDistributor
documentation:
Distributes a set of FiniteIntervalDomain
The distribution strategy is the following:
 - the smallest domain of size > 1 is picked
 - if its max_length is greater than its min_length, a subdomain if size
   min_length is distributed, with the same boundaries
 - otherwise, a subdomain [lowestMin, lowestMax[ is distributed
Distributors

documentation: Create new repository using the distributor and self

methods.name: logilab.constraint.propagation.Repository.consistency
documentation:
Prunes the domains of the variables
This method calls constraint.narrow() and queues constraints
that are affected by recent changes in the domains.
Returns True if a solution was found
alf 20041216 -- I tried the following to avoid the cost of the
creation of new Repository objects. It resulted in functional, but
slightly slower code. If you want to try to improve it, I keep the
commented out version in the source, but the version above stays
active as it is both simpler and faster.
backup_constraints = self._constraints[:]
for domains in distributor.distribute(self._domains, verbose):
self._domains = domains
self._constraints = []
self._queue = []
for var in self._variables:
self._variableListeners[var] = []
for constraint in backup_constraints:
self.addConstraint(constraint)
yield self

Stores variables, domains and constraints
Propagates domain changes to constraints
Manages the constraint evaluation queue
